text.title: Stakeholder Conflict Facilitation Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that convert a stakeholder-conflict meeting into a structured, signed decision artefact.

rule.statement: The meeting MUST run exactly five phases with explicit timeboxes: (1) frame the disagreement (5 min), (2) state positions (15 min), (3) apply rubric (15 min), (4) decide (10 min), (5) capture dissent + assignments (15 min). The facilitator MUST announce phase boundaries verbally and MUST NOT skip a phase even when the room "agrees early".
rule.rationale: Skipping the rubric phase when "we already agree" is exactly when soft consensus papers over real disagreement — the conflict re-emerges two weeks later. Forcing every phase preserves the artefact integrity regardless of room dynamics.

rule.statement: Agenda AND ground rules MUST be circulated to all attendees at least 24 hours before the meeting. Ground rules MUST include at minimum: one speaker at a time, no decision before phase 4, dissent will be recorded by name.
rule.rationale: "Dissent will be recorded by name" pre-announced changes behaviour: people argue more substantively when they know their name attaches to their position. Surprise-announcing the rule mid-meeting feels like a trap and damages future cooperation.

rule.statement: Phase 3 MUST score each option using the pre-agreed rubric (criteria + weights + 1-5 scoring), live in the room, with scores read out and recorded. Narrative-only ("on balance, I prefer option B") is not a substitute for scoring.
rule.rationale: The rubric forces the implicit weighing into the open: two stakeholders who "both like option B" often score it very differently, and the difference is the real conversation. Pure narrative facilitation lets the senior voice dominate.

rule.statement: Any stakeholder who voted against the chosen option MUST have their dissent captured with name + one-sentence grounds + "I commit to the chosen option" or "I escalate" choice. Anonymising the dissent ("some attendees disagreed") or omitting it is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Named dissent + commitment-or-escalation is the disagree-and-commit primitive — without it, dissenters re-open the decision unofficially later. Recording the commit closes the loop; recording the escalation routes the conflict cleanly upward.

rule.statement: Output MUST be a one-page decision log entry filed in the project's decision register (`decisions/YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.md`), containing: framing, positions, rubric scores, decision, dissent, named owner for each assignment, sign-off line per attendee. The entry is published within 1 working day of the meeting.
rule.rationale: An undocumented or late-documented decision is a decision in name only — within a week, attendees remember different things. Same-day publishing locks the artefact while memory is fresh and gives dissenters a chance to challenge the wording before it sets.

example.code:
# Decision: 2026-05-20-checkout-discount-rule
## Framing Disagreement on whether discount codes apply to subscription products. Sources: Maria (VP Ops) — yes; Tomás (CFO) — no.
## Positions (phase 2) - Maria: discounts drive trial-to-paid; finance can absorb 5% lift. - Tomás: subscription LTV math breaks if base price is discountable.
## Rubric scores (phase 3) | Criterion | Weight | Option A (yes) | Option B (no) | |--------------------|--------|-----------------|----------------| | Revenue impact | 0.4 | 3 | 4 | | Conversion lift | 0.3 | 5 | 2 | | Operational cost | 0.2 | 3 | 4 | | Brand consistency | 0.1 | 4 | 4 | | Weighted total | | 3.7 | 3.4 |
## Decision (phase 4) Option A — discounts apply to subscriptions, capped at 15%.
## Dissent (phase 5) - Tomás: "Disagree on LTV grounds. I commit to the chosen option, revisit at next QBR if churn rises."
## Sign-off - Maria Lopes — 2026-05-20T11:40Z - Tomás Silva — 2026-05-20T11:41Z - BA: Anna Mendes — 2026-05-20T11:42Z
example.note: All five phases produced an artefact. Dissent named, committed, scheduled for revisit. The decision is enforceable and reviewable.

example.code:
# Notes from meeting
Discussed discount codes for subscriptions. Decided to allow them. Some folks weren't fully convinced but agreed to move on.
example.note: No phases, no rubric, no named dissent, no sign-off. "Agreed to move on" guarantees the topic re-opens within two sprints.
